sentences.base_language: I went for a run this morning.
sentences.target_language: 今朝ランニングをしました。
sentences.grammarNote: 今朝 (kesa) = 'this morning'. Japanese has specific words for relative times: 今朝 (this morning), 今夜 (tonight), 昨夜 (last night). These don't require a particle; they stand alone as time expressions without に.

〜間 (kan) attached to a time word expresses duration: 三十分間 (for thirty minutes), 一時間 (for one hour). For shorter actions, 間 is sometimes dropped: 三十分走った. Compare with a point in time (三十分に) which uses に.

筋肉痛 (kinnikutsū) = 'muscle soreness / DOMS'. In Japanese, the body part is the subject with が: 脚が痛い (my legs hurt). No possessive 'my' is needed — the subject-object relationship is clear from context, as with Chinese.

sentences.target_language: 走った後にストレッチをした方がいいです。
sentences.romanized: Hashitta ato ni sutorecchi wo shita hō ga ii desu.
sentences.literal: Ran after stretching do better is.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜た方がいい (ta hō ga ii) = 'it would be better to / you should'. It follows the past tense form: した方がいい (better to do), 行った方がいい (better to go). It's softer than a direct command and common for giving advice.

sentences.base_language: I take a shower after exercising.
sentences.target_language: 運動した後にシャワーを浴びます。
sentences.romanized: Undō shita ato ni shawā wo abimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: シャワーを浴びる (shawā wo abiru) = 'to take a shower' — literally 'to bathe in a shower'. お風呂に入る (ofuro ni hairu) = 'to take a bath'. These specific verb-noun collocations are fixed in Japanese and differ from English.

sentences.target_language: 運動すると体が健康になります。
sentences.romanized: Undō suru to karada ga kenkō ni narimasu.
sentences.literal: Exercise do when body healthy becomes.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜と (to) as a conditional: 'when / if you do X, Y naturally follows'. 運動すると健康になる = 'when you exercise, you become healthy'. になる (ni naru) = 'to become': 健康になる (become healthy), 上手になる (become skilled).

sentences.base_language: I try to exercise at least three times a week.
sentences.target_language: 少なくとも週に三回は運動するようにしています。
sentences.romanized: Sukunakutomo shū ni sankai wa undō suru yō ni shite imasu.
sentences.literal: At-least per-week three-times exercise do-so-as trying.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜ようにしている (yō ni shite iru) = 'make a habit of / try to regularly do'. It expresses an ongoing effort or rule you set for yourself. 少なくとも (sukunakutomo) = 'at least'. 〜回 (kai) is the counter for occurrences: 三回 (three times).

sentences.target_language: 今日は体の調子がいいです。
sentences.romanized: Kyō wa karada no chōshi ga ii desu.
